After the column ended she moved to The Listener's digital edition and launched an independent Substack. She has authored 'Fake', optioned for Paramount+ Australia.

andrea-vance-stuff.name: Andrea Vance

connections.role: Formerly Fairfax NZ
United Future leader and Revenue Minister — Textgate 2013 (86 emails in 13 days; Dunne resigned; Vance's phone records and parliamentary swipe card data were improperly accessed)

facts.text: 'Textgate' (2013): exchanged 86 emails over 13 days with Revenue Minister Peter Dunne about GCSB leak inquiry; Dunne resigned; a subsequent inquiry improperly accessed Vance's phone records AND parliamentary swipe card movement data — a major NZ privacy scandal

andrea-vance-stuff.bio: Andrea Vance is a Northern Irish political journalist currently serving as a senior journalist at Stuff in Wellington. She grew up near Belfast, where her father edited three local newspapers. She worked for seven years as an investigative reporter at the British tabloid News of the World. When the phone hacking scandal broke, Vance was in the Glasgow newsroom and has denied any personal involvement, stating "no one had any idea it was going on." She subsequently worked as night news editor at The Scotsman before emigrating to New Zealand with her Kiwi husband. She became a prominent press gallery journalist, working for Fairfax/Stuff and later 1News. In 2013, she was at the centre of the Peter Dunne email scandal, in which minister Dunne resigned after refusing to release 86 email exchanges with Vance regarding a leaked GCSB report. Her phone records and parliamentary swipe-card data were improperly provided to the inquiry without her knowledge, prompting a major privacy breach complaint. She was named Political Journalist of the Year in 2025.
andrea-vance-stuff.methodology: Based on 30 articles scored by AI

anna-fifield-stuff.name: Anna Fifield

connections.role: Formerly Fairfax NZ

anna-fifield-stuff.bio: Anna Fifield (born 1976, Hastings) is a New Zealand journalist with an extensive international career. She studied English at Victoria University of Wellington and completed a postgraduate journalism diploma at the University of Canterbury. At 25, she moved to London and joined the Financial Times, where she spent 13 years as a foreign correspondent covering the Middle East, Korea, and US politics. She joined The Washington Post in 2014 as Tokyo bureau chief, later becoming Beijing bureau chief. She won the 2018 Shorenstein Journalism Award from Stanford and authored "The Great Successor" (2019), a book on Kim Jong Un translated into 24 languages. She was also a Nieman Fellow at Harvard. Returning to New Zealand in 2020, she edited The Dominion Post/Stuff's Wellington newsroom until December 2022. She returned to the Washington Post as Asia-Pacific editor but was laid off in February 2026. In March 2026, she joined The Spinoff to write the World Bulletin newsletter.
anna-fifield-stuff.methodology: Based on 27 articles scored by AI

audrey-young-nzherald.name: Audrey Young

connections.role: Political editor NZ Herald

audrey-young-nzherald.bio: Audrey Young is one of New Zealand's most experienced political journalists. She began her career as a sub-editor at the Auckland Sun, and after its closure in 1988 joined the New Zealand Herald as a sub-editor. She rose through the ranks to become the Herald's political editor, a role she held for 18 years (2003-2021), leading the paper's press gallery team in Wellington. In 2021, she transitioned to the role of Senior Political Correspondent, allowing her to focus on in-depth reporting. She was named Political Journalist of the Year at the Voyager Media Awards in 2018, 2020, and 2023. Her 30th anniversary at the Herald was acknowledged in Parliament. She has a particular interest in foreign affairs and Treaty of Waitangi issues, and served for six years as the union representative on the Press Council.

barry-soper-newstalkzb.bio: Barry Soper (born c. 1951, Gore) is the longest-serving member of New Zealand's Parliamentary Press Gallery, having joined in 1980. After briefly attending the Royal New Zealand Police College in 1969, he entered journalism at The Southland Times, progressing through the Mataura Ensign, Otago Daily Times, New Zealand Truth, and Waikato Times. He joined the press gallery working for APN before moving to Newstalk ZB, where he has served as political editor and senior political correspondent. He has covered eleven prime ministers, both Fijian coups (1987 and 2000), and Nelson Mandela's inauguration. He was named Individual Radio Journalist of the Year in 2001. In 1992, he was approached to stand as a Labour Party candidate in the Wellington Central by-election but declined. He married journalist and broadcaster Heather du Plessis-Allan in 2009. He was named unfavourably in Jacinda Ardern's 2025 memoir.

bernard-hickey-thespinoff.name: Bernard Hickey

connections.role: Economics columnist

bernard-hickey-thespinoff.bio: Bernard Hickey is a Wellington-based financial journalist, editor, and commentator with over two decades of experience spanning Reuters, the Financial Times Group, and Fairfax Media, working across London, Singapore, and Sydney. He co-founded and edited Interest.co.nz and Newsroom Pro. He is best known for an acknowledged ideological shift over his career: initially a conventional free-market economics commentator, he moved substantially leftward, becoming a vocal critic of New Zealand's housing policies, wealth inequality, and what he characterises as the failure of neoliberal settings to deliver for younger generations. His podcast with The Spinoff, "When the Facts Change" (named after the Keynes aphorism), reflects this evolution. He founded The Kaka, a Substack newsletter covering political economy, housing, climate, and poverty.

bryce-edwards-stuff.bio: Bryce Edwards is a political commentator and academic based at Victoria University of Wellington, where he is a Research Associate in the Institute of Governance and Policy Studies and Director of the Democracy Project. He holds a PhD in political sociology (2004) and previously lectured in Political Studies at Otago University. Edwards has a well-documented history on the political left. He helped found the Canterbury University Progressive Left Club in 1993 and was a contact for the Canterbury University Marxist Society in 1995. He worked in the Alliance Party's electoral liaison unit in 2001 as an executive assistant. In 2007, he was identified as a member of the Workers Party and spoke at the Marxism 2007 conference. Despite this left-wing background, he has built a mainstream media career as a political analyst, writing regular columns for the NZ Herald, The Guardian, and other outlets.

mike-hosking-newstalkzb.bio: Mike Hosking (born 1965, Dunedin) is New Zealand's most prominent radio broadcaster. He left school at 16, starting as an advertising copywriter before getting his break on Wellington's Radio Windy. He hosted Radio New Zealand's Tonight Show and Morning Report, then joined Newstalk ZB in 2008 as weekday Breakfast host — a role he still holds. On television, he co-presented TVNZ's Breakfast (1997-2005) and Seven Sharp (2014-2017). He writes a regular opinion column for the NZ Herald. Hosking is an open supporter of the centre-right National Party and is frequently critical of Labour-led governments. His career has drawn multiple successful Broadcasting Standards Authority complaints. He is married to fellow Newstalk ZB broadcaster Kate Hawkesby. A landmark 2003 privacy case involving Hosking established "the Hosking principles" in NZ common law.

ryan-bridge-newstalkzb.name: Ryan Bridge

connections.role: Newstalk ZB broadcaster

ryan-bridge-newstalkzb.bio: Ryan Bridge (born November 1987, Kapiti Coast) is a New Zealand broadcaster and journalist. He began as a political reporter in the parliamentary press gallery for RNZ, later becoming TV3's Beijing correspondent and co-host of AM on Three. When Newshub closed in 2024, he moved to NZME. He publicly came out as gay in 2021. In May 2025, he launched Herald NOW, a live-streamed morning news show. He hosts Early Edition (5-6am weekdays) on Newstalk ZB and Herald NOW on NZME.

simon-wilson-nzherald.name: Simon Wilson

connections.role: Senior writer at NZ Herald (from 2018) and The Listener

simon-wilson-nzherald.bio: Simon Wilson is an award-winning senior writer at the NZ Herald covering politics, climate, transport, housing, and urban design. He was formerly editor of Metro magazine and has worked in magazines since the mid-1980s. He authored 'Homeground' (2022) about the Auckland City Mission and the novel 'The Age of Light' (1994). He is the first non-fiction writer to win the Nigel Cox Unity Books Award.

thomas-coughlan-nzherald.bio: Thomas Coughlan is the political editor of the New Zealand Herald. He holds a Master's degree in Political Thought and Intellectual History from the University of Cambridge (2014-2015). Coughlan joined the Parliamentary Press Gallery in 2018, working first for Newsroom and Stuff before moving to the NZ Herald in 2021.

toby-manhire-thespinoff.name: Toby Manhire

connections.role: Editor-at-large

toby-manhire-thespinoff.bio: Toby Manhire is a New Zealand journalist, editor, and the son of Bill Manhire, New Zealand's inaugural Poet Laureate. He edited student magazine Salient in 1997 before spending over a decade in London, where he worked at The Guardian, including as editor of its comment pages. He is editor-at-large at The Spinoff and host of Gone By Lunchtime, a popular politics podcast alongside Annabelle Lee-Mather and Ben Thomas.

tova-obrien-1news.name: Tova O'Brien

connections.role: Co-host of TVNZ Breakfast (from April 2026)

tova-obrien-1news.bio: Tova O'Brien (born c. 1982-83) is one of New Zealand's highest-profile political journalists. Born in Papua New Guinea, she began at TV3/MediaWorks in 2007, rising to Newshub's political editor in 2018. She won Political Journalist of the Year (2019). She briefly moved to Today FM in 2022 before it folded, then joined Stuff. In December 2025, TVNZ announced O'Brien would join Breakfast as co-host from April 2026.
